video_title: The Steam Deck Has a Problem... (in 2026)
comment_text:
The Steam Deck is the best console I've bought since the PS2.  
The retro compatibility is insane; the user community is so well established that every issue I encounter has an answer on the internet. 
The Deck can also please tinkerers like me and casual gamers just as much.
Most optimized games can run between 30-45 fps, and there are so many amazing developer tools and plugins that add to its already good features. 
The Steam customer service is well regarded, and the Steam store has a vast catalog of games with amazing discounts throughout the year. 
Steam is still constantly updating the handheld. 
The Steam Deck made me love Linux, play on the couch, and mod my games so much more that my PS5 and other consoles have been gathering dust ever since.
cleaned_comment_text: the steam deck is the best console i've bought since the ps2. the retro compatibility is insane; the user community is so well established that every issue i encounter has an answer on the internet. the deck can also please tinkerers like me and casual gamers just as much. most optimized games can run between 30-45 fps, and there are so many amazing developer tools and plugins that add to its already good features. the steam customer service is well regarded, and the steam store has a vast catalog of games with amazing discounts throughout the year. steam is still constantly updating the handheld. the steam deck made me love linux, play on the couch, and mod my games so much more that my ps5 and other consoles have been gathering dust ever since.

comment_text: I upgraded from the iPhone 13 Mini to the 17. But I found that the 13 Mini fits perfectly in my shirt pocket while the 17 doesn’t, so I always have the Mini with me. It has remained my main driver. It (still) does everything I really need. The 17 is now my backup phone, nice to have on the couch but not in my pocket. I appreciate the added memory, the 120 Hz display, the longer battery life, the Dynamic Island, etc but to have my iPhone on hand, all day long, is the overriding advantage, as far as I am concerned.

video_title: Nintendo Switch 2 Review - Best Worst Console Ever.
comment_text:
I feel like looking at it as a console is the wrong view; they were never in to compete with PS or Xbox. It is a Gameboy that you can comfortably play on a TV. And that was absolutely genius move by Nintendo.

As a PC gamer with more power than any console will have for a long time, the Switch has been the perfect system: there when I want to pick it up and go and there when I want to melt into my couch. I don’t buy the “pretty” games for it (I frankly don’t buy them much at all; AAA is pretty sh*t these days), I get the niche, usually pixel art, indie stuff, a few Nintendo titles, and retro SNES and Genesis games. There are a few outliers that are a real pleasure on the Switch with my surround system, like Ori.

I did finally just get a proper controller for it (PDP, which should work with the Switch 2 I have coming) and I will say that makes a huge difference. I’m actually finally enjoying BoTW.

My Switch will probably be retired to one of my desk monitors and for out-of-home use until one of my nephews is old enough to not break everything they touch. 😆

video_title: I Can No Longer Recommend the Steam Deck...
comment_text:
I can't recommend getting any new video game hardware right now. Prices going up made things too volatile. I'd say stick with what you have right now, console or PC. All I can recommend are retro emulation handhelds like the stuff from Anbernic as well as Android handhelds like the AYN Odin 3, but even those will likely see price increases.

Maybe the Xbox ROG Ally (standard version, not X). As expensive as that is, it's way cheaper and way more powerful than the Steam Deck, although not as powerful as the Ally X. It has a much better price-to-performance ratio than the Steam Deck now.

I would also consider checking out the used market if you're looking for PC parts, but watch out for scalpers.

When buying a gaming PC or a console, you have to take everything into consideration. "What can I do with my machine?" "How's the price-to-performance ratio?" "Do the amount of games make this machine worth buying?" "Will it last me a long time?"

Things are very bad right now.

video_title: That's It...I can't recommend the Steam Deck anymore.
comment_text:
The best value is a legion go 1 which you can obtain used for $500. Then if you install SteamOS onto it, it will be able to run most games at a resolution of 1600p using some combination of in game FSR & the Linux port of lossless scaling. You will also be able to exceed 100 fps on most games by scaling down your resolution & running lossless scaling. But that 1600p resolution is twice the resolution of a steam deck. I just played both 007 First Light at 1600p and Mafia the Old Country at 1600p on a SteamOS legion go 1. You will exceed 30 FPS minimum. But you’ll likely be able to push it up to 60fps tbh. At 1600p.

If you understand how to use performance tools/enhancement tech. If you know how to utilize basic performance cheat tools then Windows absolutely thrashes SteamOS in performance. For me the only real draw to SteamOS on a portable is the ability to run games at 1600p with just whatever FRS is included in game. And not having to upscale with AMD software to reach that resolution. So the legion go 1 is easily the #1 portable to run SteamOS off of. No question.

video_title: 💞 Nintendo switch unboxing ✨🎀
Nintendo Switch 2: A Pricey Disappointment?
comment_text: First, the Switch 2 or Steam Deck or ROG Ally should not be compared directly with a home console like the PS5 or Xbox. None of them are designed to deliver the level of performance comparable to home console. The Switch 2’s success is driven less by raw power and more by its combination of portability, convenience, and strong first-party exclusives. It is not simply a case of the hardware being underpowered. In fact, the Switch 2 is capable enough to handle demanding titles like Resident Evil 9 and Pragmata better than the Steam Deck. Look up YouTube videos. The bigger limitation is often whether studios choose to release their games on Nintendo hardware at all, or whether older titles receive proper updates and optimization for the new system. That is similar to how there are still many PlayStation-exclusive titles that you cannot access on the Steam Deck. That said more third-party titles are now making their way to the Switch 2.
